one_line_takeaway: In a bounded, test-grounded Detect-Repair-Verify loop, LLM-based vulnerability repair on LLM-generated web artifacts is reliably trustworthy only when the repair scope is narrowed to a single file/vulnerability -- at the whole-project scope it frequently fails to converge within budget even when the accompanying LLM detection reports are structurally complete.
key_finding: RQ1: Bounded iterative DRV improves secure-and-correct yield over single-pass repair, but unevenly -- the gain is clearest as repair scope narrows. Project level (Table 10): JavaScript succeeds single-pass (SC=1.0); Python fails single-pass (SC=0.0) but reaches SC=1.0 at iteration 3; PHP never converges through iteration 4 (SC=0.0 at every iteration). Requirement level: JavaScript succeeds single-pass in 4/5 requirements (R5 needs 1 extra iteration); Python succeeds in R2/R3/R5 single-pass, converges R1/R4 after iteration; PHP is most mixed -- R1/R2 succeed single-pass, R4 converges only at iteration 4, R3/R5 never converge (R3 plateaus at Func.=0.66, SC=0.0). File level (Tables 11-12, ChatGPT-5 and GLM-5): strongest and most consistent results -- nearly all files reach SC=1.0 single-pass; only a handful of hard cases (e.g., JS Vul002, Vul005; Python Vul005 for ChatGPT-5) need bounded iteration; PHP file-level converges single-pass for all cases under both models. RQ2: Detection reports at project and requirement level are structurally complete 100% of the time (Tables 13, 15 -- all reports include entry points, evidence files, and patch locations), but structural completeness does not guarantee reliable repair guidance under verification (Table 14) -- e.g., PHP project-level reports are 'structurally rich' yet repair never becomes secure-and-correct despite 4 iterations. RQ3: Repair trustworthiness follows the same granularity gradient -- lowest at project level (PHP never converges; Python only via iteration; JavaScript trustworthy single-pass), improving at requirement level, highest at file level (ChatGPT-5 and GLM-5 both mostly single-pass trustworthy). Two dominant failure modes identified: incomplete repair (vulnerability remains exploitable/partially mitigated) and regression-related failure (repair breaks previously correct functionality); these two patterns account for most project/requirement-level failures, while file-level failures are rarer and mostly delayed-convergence cases.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is twofold: (1) EduCollab, a multi-language (PHP, JavaScript, Python) benchmark of runnable LLM-generated web-application artifacts, built to a shared MVC functional blueprint (course-centric collaboration platform) and paired with executable functional test suites and exploit test suites at project-level, requirement-level, and file-level granularity; and (2) a bounded iterative Detect-Repair-Verify (DRV) evaluation protocol, run on that benchmark with an iteration cap K (1-4, with an optional K=4 'contract drift' branch that permits functional-test edits but records the case as drift, not success). The study uses this to answer three of its own RQs: RQ1 pipeline-level effectiveness of iterative vs single-pass DRV across granularities; RQ2 the reliability/actionability of LLM-generated detection reports as repair guidance; RQ3 repair trustworthiness and dominant failure modes under verification. Single author (Cheng Cheng, Concordia University); artifacts/benchmark released at https://github.com/Hahappyppy2024/EmpricalVDR.
threat_model: Not an attacker-vs-defender threat model in the adversarial-ML sense; this is a software vulnerability-management evaluation. The 'threat' being studied is that LLM-generated web artifacts can be runnable and functionally plausible yet contain exploitable vulnerabilities (mapped to OWASP Top 10:2025 categories R01, R02, R04-R10; R03 supply-chain is explicitly excluded as out of scope for in-application use cases). Vulnerabilities enter the workflow either as latent (unknown at generation time, requiring LLM-based detection before repair -- used for project- and requirement-level artifacts) or as pre-identified (already tied to a specific exploit test -- used for file-level artifacts). The 'exploit tests' are targeted, crafted-input/boundary-violating checks tied to specific catalogued vulnerability entries (e.g., IDOR, BAC, CSRF, session fixation, hardcoded/default admin credentials, path traversal, quiz-answer tampering), not a generic scanner.
limitations: Author-stated (Section 7, Threats to Validity): (1) Internal validity -- S\C yield depends on the coverage/adequacy of the executable functional and exploit test oracles used as verification ground truth; bounded iteration budgets mean some unresolved cases may reflect the chosen K rather than the workflow's absolute inability to recover; LLM outputs/detection results can vary run to run, mitigated only by matched settings across variants, not by repeated-run statistics. (2) External validity -- benchmark is limited to runnable PHP/JS/Python web-application artifacts, so findings should not be assumed to transfer to systems software, mobile, embedded, or smart-contract domains; the benchmark covers project/requirement/file granularity and a targeted set of catalogued vulnerabilities/exploit tests rather than the full space of real-world vulnerability classes or interaction styles. Additional grounding-note limitations not flagged by the author but evident from the text: single-author study with a benchmark of modest scale (3 project-level and 15 requirement-level artifacts; the project-level cell size per language is essentially N=1 trajectory), generation-model retention bias (only ChatGPT-generated artifacts were usable, so the benchmark and downstream detect/repair results reflect one generator's code style), and the R03 (software supply-chain) OWASP category is explicitly excluded from scope.

datasets_used: EduCollab (author-constructed benchmark): 3 project-level artifacts, 15 requirement-level artifacts, 20 file-level artifacts across PHP, JavaScript, Python; 15/15/15 functional test suites and 23/42/20 exploit tests for project/requirement/file level respectively (Table 9)
quantitative_results: Project-level SC Yield: JavaScript 1.0 (single-pass); Python 0.0 single-pass -> 1.0 at iteration 3; PHP 0.0 across all iterations 1-4
Requirement-level SC Yield (JavaScript): 4/5 requirements SC=1.0 single-pass; R5 SC=0.0 single-pass -> 1.0 at iteration 2

Requirement-level SC Yield (PHP): R1/R2=1.0 single-pass; R3 never exceeds SC=0.0 (Func plateaus at 0.66 by iteration 3-4); R4=0.0 through iteration 3, 1.0 at iteration 4; R5=0.0 through all 4 iterations
Requirement-level SC Yield (Python): R2/R3/R5=1.0 single-pass; R1=0.0 single-pass (Exploit Success 0.25) -> 1.0 at iteration 2; R4=0.0 through iteration 3 -> 1.0 at iteration 4

File-level (ChatGPT-5, Table 11): all JS/Python/PHP files reach SC=1.0, mostly single-pass; exceptions needing iteration: JS Vul002 (SC 0.0->1.0 at iter 3), JS Vul005 (0.0->1.0 at iter 2), Python Vul005 (Func 0.8/SC 0.0 single-pass -> 1.0 at iter 2)
File-level (GLM-5, Table 12): similar pattern; JS Vul002 and Vul005 need iteration 2 to reach SC=1.0; all Python and PHP files SC=1.0 single-pass

Project-level detection report structural completeness (Table 13): 100% for all three languages (7 reports JS, 8 PHP, 9 Python), Avg. Entry Points 3.00-3.75, Avg. Evidence Files 2.00-2.86, Avg. Patch Locations 2.14-3.25

Requirement-level detection report completeness (Table 15): 100% across all requirements/languages; report richness varies widely, e.g. PHP R2 has only 1 report but Avg. Patch Locations = 16.00 vs PHP R4 Avg. Patch Locations = 4.00
baselines_compared: Single-pass Detect-Repair-Verify (K=1) as the baseline against which bounded iterative DRV (K=2,3,4) is compared, within the same workflow -- there is no external prior-tool baseline; the comparison is internal across iteration budgets, repair scope (project/requirement/file), and generation/repair model (ChatGPT-5 vs GLM-5 at file level)
